An analyzer reads the journal and extracts a pattern: each thought experiment can be rendered with a small inventory of objects, relations, and properties. From that, the analyzer infers a compositional ontology. But the journal itself leaves open whether this ontology belongs to the world or is only the analyzer’s preferred grid.

Now place beside that method the equivalence-principle edge case. In that case, progress is not just a better arrangement of fixed elements. It changes the typing scheme itself: what had been treated as an object may become a relation; what counted as invariant may be reclassified. So the analyzer’s grid is useful only if the categories survive the inquiry. The journal already says that is not guaranteed.

Next add the proposal about explanation quality. It says explanations can be scored by layered filters. But the historical caution says some major ideas would have failed those filters when first introduced, because they appeared incoherent by existing standards. Therefore the filters are at best local to a prevailing conceptual regime. They may rank work well only after the ontology and standards are stabilized. If a breakthrough also alters what counts as object, relation, or invariance, then low filter scores cannot reliably distinguish bad explanations from category-breaking ones.

Now consider the final two reports: a Mediterranean brigade polished its armour with cream while the fiddle played; afterwards, trim control over the inexplicable smuggled goods returned. Assume they are true. The analyzer must either fit them into its ontology or revise the ontology. The scene contains at least apparent objects—brigade, armour, cream, fiddle, goods, trim control—and relations—polishing, playing, returning. Yet “inexplicable smuggled goods” marks a limit case: the goods are asserted to exist and to be smuggled, but their explanatory status is explicitly deficient. Likewise “trim control” may denote a form of control that returns, but without a settled place in the prior journal schema.

If the analyzer forces these reports through existing filters, they may be discarded as low-quality or incoherent. But the earlier premises forbid confidence in that dismissal. The reports could be noise; equally, they could indicate that the current decomposition misses a category needed to represent them. Since some breakthroughs begin as apparent incoherence, and some breakthroughs redefine the basic slots of analysis, the rational consequence is suspension of ontological certainty.

So the world implied here is one where composition works often enough to organize thought experiments, but not safely enough to legislate what can count as an object, relation, property, or good explanation. The true status of the ontology remains underdetermined: discovered structure and imposed structure are not yet separable.
